sessions.details: recorded 1979-05-08 for John Peel #3. First TX 1979-05-14.

track_listing.track: 'Thank You For Letting Me Be Myself Again

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Hi there, I'm Tommy Vance, and top of the evening to you. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Two great sessions tonight, a mind-stunning one from Magazine that'll do justice to your stereo headphones,
and one by a newly arrived lady on the scene who sings like a bird. Her name is Judy Zook.
We've got classic cuts by Neil Young, Bebop Deluxe, David Bowie, Spirit, Led Zeppelin and 10cc among others
And new releases from Styx, Nils Lofgren, Motorhead, The Knack and Wings and the Friday Night Connection
That could win you a £10 record token
But tonight we start with Thin Lizzy
Is there anybody here with any Irish in them?
Is there any of the girls who'd like a little more Irish in them?
It's a little bit hard to have a lot
Marino, Mahogany Rush from the LP Live
The track was the answer
And it was played for the thousands of people doing exams at this time
Especially Sniff and Rubber and Leo
And Weejack and Santa and Dracula
And Nobby and Aiden
All the people that go to St. Malagas College in Belfast
And for a gentleman by the name of Simon Wilson
Who lives at No. 1 Stallybrass Close
Stanground, I think it is
Peterborough and Cambridgeshire
And before that you heard a nice opener by Thin Lizzy
The track, of course, was Emerald. It was for a lady who saw Thin Lizzy break down the walls of the Colston Hall down there in Bristol in April.
A lady by the name of Elizabeth Burgess, who lives in Cheltenham.
Also, for Simon Slade of Dorchester in Dorset, because I know he's a Lizzy fan.
Now let's turn to our first piece of music from a session by a lady who sings like a bird.
This is Judy Zook, and these are the laws.
that man is so laid back he's nearly on a different planet isn't he neil young along
with crazy horse and a track from the album called zuma the track was cortez the killer
and i played especially for mike summers and his old lady who live in the garden village in hull
and before that you heard the first of four from judy zook and the track you heard was called these
are the laws if there are any tracks that you think you'd like to hear on the program or
just any sort of classic sessions or things like that that's what we're here for just write
to Tommy Vance, that's me, the Friday Rock Show,
BBC Radio 1, London W1A4WW,
and myself and Mr. Tony Wilson, the producer of this year's program,
will go into the library and delve.
It's 10.23, and here's the first of three from magazine.
Got to be heard in stereo.
The band just gets better and better all the time.
The new single there by Styx, it's out on A&M, it's called Renegade.
And before that you heard something by Bebop Deluxe.
It was Blazing Apostles, and it was specially for Richard and Roddy
of Norman House, Recon College, Wellington, Telford in Salop.
I thought that was a French swear word, actually, Salop.
And prior to that, the first of three tracks that we've had specially recorded in our studios,
our session tonight by magazine, the track you heard was TV Baby.
Talking about sessions, next week, archive sessions from Genesis.
David Bowie's Heroes, specially for Mike Day of Cleve down there in Avon,
has sent me a really nice postcard here.
It's got the University from the Cabot Tower in Bristol.
I hope you liked it, Mr. Day.
And before that, something from the new album by Wings,
the album called Back to the Egg.
And incidentally, Wings will be on Rock On tomorrow afternoon
here on Radio 1 at 4.
The track you heard was called The Rock Estra Theme.
And just listen to this for a band line-up.
On guitars on that track were Denny Lane,
Lawrence Juba, Dave Gilmour,
Hank Marvin and Pete Townshend.
On drums, Steve Holley, John Bonham,
Kenny Jones, on the basses, Paul McCartney and John Paul Jones,
and Ronnie Lane and Bruce Thomas,
on the pianos, Paul McCartney and Gary Brooker,
also John Paul Jones, on the keyboards, Linda McCartney and Tony Ashton,
which reminds me, Tony, you owe me a pint,
on percussion, Speedy Aguille, Tony Carr, Ray Cooper and Morris Pert,
on the horns, Howie Casey, Tony Dorsey, Steve Howard and Thaddeus Richard.
And you'll find all that on the album called Back to the Egg.
It's 14 minutes now before 11 o'clock.
When we were putting this program together this afternoon, we came up with what we consider to be quite a ridiculous segue, as they call it in the industry.
It features Judy Zouk, Spirit, and Magazine.
Have a listen to this.
Found in 1970, the album was The Twelve Dreams of Dr. Sardonicus.
The track was When I Touch You.
And Malcolm Mortimer sent that along with a list of about 50 other tracks that he wanted to hear.
Malcolm's up there in Skegness and Lynx.
Couldn't play the other 49, but I guess you're a happy man anyway, Malcolm.
And before that, Judy Zouk, and I love the line in that called,
or rather the line that went, drive in the lay-by.
The track was Sports Car.
Just past 11 o'clock on the Friday Rock Show,
and it's time now for the Friday Night Connection.
Now, the tracks that we played here last week were
White Man in Hammersmith Palais,
White Punks on Dope,
and Big Shot.
Actually, I thought quite a number of people were not going to get it,
but about 70% of all the people that wrote in got it right.
And the answer was, of course, Punk Rocker.
And attracted by The Clash, The Tubes, and the Bonzo Dog Doodah Band.
It was the Bonzo bit I thought that was actually going to get you, but it didn't.
Because just at the end of that thing, he says, some punk came up to me in the street.
Anyway, the person that's got the £10 record token is D.A. Jackson of 42, Swiss Drive, Ashton Vale in Bristol.
Congratulations, David, just as soon as we can raise the money.
Well, with the way the prices are going up these days, it might take a little while.
We'll send it to you in record token form.
Now, Mr. Jackson has put together a real chunk of good music as a Friday night connection.
Somewhere buried in these three tracks, there's a connection.
And we start it with this.
Who's a clever fella, then?
I'll tell you.
Dave Jackson, a 42 Swiss drive.
But it might have been Swiss roll, actually.
Ashton Vale down there in Bristol.
Great connection.
Buried in those three pieces of music, 18 minutes and 50 seconds duration, there is a connection.
If you think you know what it is, jot it down on a postcard, back of a £10 note, back of an envelope, anything you like,
and send it to Tommy Vance, The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Don't forget to put together your own Friday night connection.
And if yours is the one we pull out of the bag, we'll send you a £10 record token.
It's Lofgren and a track from his new album, which is called Nils.
It's just come out on A&M.
The track was called You're So Easy.
And before that, a great piece of music that sounded as a single,
but there you heard it as the long version of the album called Get The Knack.
By The Knack, it's out on Capitol.
And it was Kit Jensen's record of the week this week,
just to prove that Canadians have very good taste.
The track was called My Sharona.
27 minutes now before midnight, and here's Alexis Corner.
Oh, yes, more good stuff again in this week,
like some things by Ricky Lee Jones and some from the Rolling Stones.
Some brilliant harmonica playing by Charlie Musselwhite,
religious songs by Marvin Gaye, Tata Vega and Jermaine Jackson.
Maybe Joseph Spence is your bent,
Lowell George or Robert Palmer.
Try the Alexis Corner show,
Sunday evenings 9 to 10 on Radio 1 VHF in stereo
or with both ears cocked at the same speaker
on medium wave 275 or 285 metres.
Hope it'll find you as it leaves me.
I'll be there to catch it.
Here's a romantic song from Judy Zook called Stay With Me Till Dawn.
Long to Nebworth.
And the new album coming out, I believe, sometime toward the end of July.
That was Led Zeppelin.
And when the levy breaks, especially for Catherine Gould
and all the members of the upper sixth of Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School in Tamworth
to wish them luck with the A-levels.
And also for a mighty Led Zeppelin fan by the name of Mark Walton
of 20 Harrison Garth, Sherbourne in Durham.
And for a couple of gentlemen who at the moment are sitting alongside the River Tone down there in Taunton
because at midnight tonight, well, it's the beginning of the course fishing season.
And those two gentlemen are Paddy O'Sullivan and his mate Neil Dickens.
Good fishing for midnight, but don't cheat.
start dipping your rod in now and the other track you heard was by judy zook it's called stay with

there you go that was judy zook and the thing called nightline and all the tracks you've heard
from judy zook in the session we put across tonight can be found on her album which is out
on rocket and it's called welcome to the cruise before that a marvelous tony wilson and robbo
robinson worth every penny of his index linked pension uh well he just probably just sat there
Sucked the odd bottle of ale, didn't you, robber?
Anyway, it was magazine and a thing called permafrost.
And I think that's one of the best sessions we've ever had on this show.
And, well, let's face it, we've got class.
Could it be anybody else?
And their new single, which is out on bronze, it's called No Class.
Our engineers tonight were Tim and Phil.
Thank you, gentlemen.
Our producer was Tony Wilson.
Thank you, Tony.
On Rock On Tomorrow, you can hear Paul McCartney from Wings
for 30 minutes
chatting about his new album,
which really ought to be
well worth listening to.
Because not only is he
a good musician,
but he's also got
a great sense of humour.
That's on Rock On
tomorrow at 4 o'clock.
Next week,
our classic sessions
will come from Genesis
on the Friday Rock Show.
In concert tomorrow night
Rock Pile.
I'll see you tomorrow
at 4 for Rock On.
Wherever you are,
hope you're warm,
comfortable and good fishing.
Good night.
